mnemonics: Complete Spaces Have No Holes: A Cauchy sequence in a complete metric space will always find a point to converge to, leaving no 'holes' or gaps.
Every Cauchy Chef Perfectly Finishes: Imagine a chef (Cauchy sequence) who follows a perfect recipe (the metric). In a complete kitchen (metric space), the dish is always finished (converges).
keyPoints: A metric space (M, d) is complete if every Cauchy sequence in M converges to a point within M.
Completeness is a property of the metric space itself, not just the metric. The same set with different metrics can be complete or incomplete.
ℝⁿ with the standard Euclidean metric is a fundamental example of a complete metric space.
A closed subset of a complete metric space is itself complete.
The Baire Category Theorem, a result about complete metric spaces, states that such a space cannot be written as a countable union of nowhere dense sets.
commonMistakes: Assuming a Cauchy sequence must converge to something, without verifying that the limit point is actually within the specific metric space in question (e.g., rational numbers with the standard metric).

Confusing a complete space with a closed space. A space is closed relative to a larger space, while completeness is an intrinsic property. A set can be closed in ℝ but not complete if given a different, non-standard metric.
Believing that completeness is a topological property (it is not; it depends on the specific metric). A homeomorphism does not necessarily preserve Cauchy sequences.
